title: Scope Management
summary: How to define, document and control exactly what the project will and will not deliver, across predictive and agile delivery. In PMBOK 8 terms, scope work lives in the Scope performance domain and its six processes: plan the work deliberately, then confirm deliverables meet scope and quality so the intended benefits are realised.

sections.heading: Where scope lives
sections.body: Scope is one of the seven PMBOK 8 performance domains, delivered through its six processes. Read the question for the delivery mode first, then answer in that mode's language.

blocks.title: How scope maps
lines: Scope domain -> organise the work deliberately: requirements, scope statement, WBS or backlog.
Scope domain -> deliverables meet scope and quality; benefits are realised.
Tailoring -> predictive fixes scope up front; agile lets scope emerge through the backlog.

groups.label: Signals
lines: Stable, well-understood scope.
Scope statement, WBS, WBS dictionary.
Change control board.

groups.label: Handle it by
lines: Lock the scope baseline.
Route any change through integrated change control.
Validate and control against the baseline.

groups.label: Signals
lines: Changing or emerging requirements.
Product backlog, user stories.
Product Owner, sprint reviews.

groups.label: Handle it by
lines: Keep a prioritised, refined backlog.
Let the Product Owner own priority and value.
Accept scope change as normal, not as a threat.

sections.heading: Product scope vs project scope
sections.body:
Product scope is the set of features and functions that characterise the deliverable - the thing the customer ends up with. Project scope is the work required to produce that deliverable, including the management activities needed to deliver it.

Measuring completeness is the giveaway. A question that talks about features satisfied is pointing at product scope; one that talks about work completed is pointing at project scope.

rows.value:
Features and functions of the deliverable.
Measured against requirements.

Work required to deliver it, plus management activities.
Measured against the scope baseline.

blocks.text: Features delivered = product scope; work performed = project scope.

sections.heading: The scope processes in order
sections.body: Predictive projects walk this sequence; agile projects fold the front half into ongoing backlog refinement, but the intent is identical - agree what is being built, then confirm it was built.

items.title: Collect Requirements
lines: Turn stakeholder needs into documented, agreed, testable statements.
Elicit via interviews, workshops and prototyping.
Agile: capture as backlog items / user stories.

items.title: Define Scope
lines: Write the project scope statement.
State what is in scope and, crucially, what is out.

items.title: Create WBS
lines: Decompose deliverables into manageable work packages.
Scope baseline = scope statement + WBS + WBS dictionary.

items.title: Validate Scope
lines: Customer or sponsor formally accepts completed deliverables.
Output: accepted deliverables.
Customer-facing.

items.title: Control Scope
lines: Monitor scope status; manage changes to the baseline.
Internal discipline that prevents creep.
Runs in parallel with Validate Scope.

blocks.title: Validate vs Control
lines: Validate = is it accepted? (customer sign-off)
Control = is scope still on baseline? (managing change)
They run together but answer different questions.

sections.heading: Requirements and the scope baseline
sections.body:
The requirements traceability matrix links every requirement back to its origin (business need or stakeholder) and forward to the deliverable, test and acceptance criterion that satisfy it. This keeps scope honest: nothing is built that no one asked for, and nothing requested is quietly dropped.

The scope baseline is the approved version of three artefacts taken together, and once approved it can only change through formal change control. WBS decomposition rules and the 100 per cent rule are covered in the Schedule and Network guide; for scope, hold that the baseline is the agreed yardstick for all later decisions.

items.text: Work Breakdown Structure: deliverable-oriented decomposition of the project into work packages.
Detail behind each WBS element: scope, deliverables, acceptance criteria.
Requirements Traceability Matrix: links each requirement to its origin, deliverable, test and acceptance criterion.
Approved scope statement + WBS + WBS dictionary, changeable only by change control.

blocks.text: If a requirement cannot be tested or traced, it is not yet a requirement.

sections.heading: Scope creep vs gold plating
sections.body: Both are unrequested work that consumes budget and schedule, adds risk and can void acceptance. They are problems, not favours. The correct response to either is the same: protect the approved scope and route any genuine change through integrated change control so it is assessed and authorised before work begins.

groups.label: What it is
lines: Scope growing in an uncontrolled way.
Changes slip in without analysis or approval.

groups.label: Pattern
lines: The steady accretion of just one more small thing.

groups.label: What it is
lines: The delivery team adds extras the customer never requested.

groups.label: Pattern
lines: Showing off or using up slack.
Still consumes budget and adds risk.

blocks.title: Same fix for both
lines: Creep is stakeholder-driven, gold plating is team-driven.
Both are wrong; both go through change control.

sections.heading: Handling change and priority
sections.body: In predictive work a baseline change is never an in-place edit; it is a request. In agile work, change is absorbed through re-prioritisation of the backlog rather than a change request. Prioritisation makes the trade-offs explicit either way.

blocks.title: Baseline change cue
lines: Scope baseline needs changing -> raise a change request.
Do not edit the baseline directly.
Assess and authorise before work begins.

rows.value: Must have / Should have / Could have / Won't have this time.
Rank requirements when scope must flex against time or budget.
Backlog ordering and refinement - see the Agile and Hybrid guide.

blocks.text: Agreed priorities steer what gets built first and what is dropped under pressure.

source: PM Cram study notes - reworded and restructured for the PMBOK 8 principle- and domain-based PMP exam.
